constraints: Reveal rooms, corridors, and connections through exploration
Do not reveal undiscovered secrets automatically
Keep objective guidance light
Avoid GPS-style navigation
Target players who enjoy skill-based top-down combat
Provide many meaningfully tailored difficulty settings for casual through mastery-focused players
Do not rely only on simple health or damage multipliers to differentiate difficulty
The complete MVP campaign, saves, progression, and difficulty modes must work offline
Optional online systems must not make the core game connectivity-dependent
Multiplayer is post-MVP and should follow only after a successful MVP
Preserve clean extension boundaries for later online and multiplayer systems without implementing their operational complexity in the MVP
Real-time cooperative campaign-style play is the first major multiplayer direction after a successful MVP
Co-op should support shared combat, exploration, objectives, and bosses
Leaderboards are also desired later but remain post-MVP
Do not implement multiplayer networking, multiplayer scaling, shared progression, or leaderboard services in the MVP
Avoid unnecessary single-player-only hard-coding where clean extension boundaries are practical
Release the polished MVP for Windows PC first
Support keyboard and mouse plus gamepad in the MVP
Android is the next intended platform after the PC version is stable
Keep gameplay actions platform-neutral and UI scalable
Suspend saves and data-driven content should remain compatible with later mobile expansion
Touch controls, mobile performance validation, and app-store release work are post-MVP
Target roughly 45 to 75 minutes for an exploratory first clear of each MVP level
Target roughly 20 to 35 minutes for a mastered run
Divide levels into natural checkpoint sections of roughly 10 to 20 minutes
Every level must remain interesting throughout
Levels should later be genuinely worth replaying and grinding rather than relying on repetitive filler
Prioritize distinct encounters, routes, secrets, objectives, progression opportunities, and mastery depth over raw floor area
Keep core layouts, bosses, major encounters, and official competitive categories deterministic
Allow optional curated modifiers, difficulty-specific enemy variants, challenge contracts, rotating bonuses, and limited reward variation
Preserve learnable mastery and fair speedrunning
Do not turn the handcrafted campaign into heavily randomized roguelike runs
Weapons are acquired primarily from level-floor pickups
Floor pickups may be represented as tiered strongboxes with random contents
Shops provide a second weapon-acquisition path through a randomized purchasable inventory
Random weapon discovery should be a central source of excitement during play
Strongboxes and shops remain genuinely random but are bounded by player progression
Duplicate weapons remain possible and can be sold or dismantled into useful value
A shop may expose up to roughly 40 randomized weapon slots and should let the player realistically buy almost any progression-appropriate weapon
Base weapon types enter the loot pool gradually as player levels unlock them
Strongboxes roll from a bounded nearby progression window rather than the full game-wide weapon pool
Eligible weapons may roll enchantments or affixes that create meaningful variants
Higher-tier boxes guarantee an appropriate minimum quality
Repeated misses may softly improve relevant odds
Limited shop rerolls or equivalent fallback mechanisms may prevent pathological unlucky streaks
The progression window, enchantments, probabilities, and power curve must be carefully balanced
Core weapon families may return at later progression milestones as stronger counterparts
Successor variants may improve statistics and introduce modest mechanical changes
Some more complex, interesting, and inherently powerful weapon types only enter the loot pool at higher levels
A basic machine gun may appear at level 1, return as an improved family counterpart around level 11, and receive another stronger variant around level 39
Deliberate power inflation is acceptable but must be planned, balanced, and paced
Early individual drops may become obsolete while their broader weapon family remains relevant through authored successors
Late-game archetypes must justify their later unlock through added complexity, novelty, or power
A shop's randomized inventory remains stable until deliberately refreshed
Players may purchase only a limited number of rerolls
Reroll costs escalate
Players may lock a small number of selected items so those items survive a refresh
The system must prevent cheap or unlimited fishing for perfect weapon rolls
Shop reroll economics must remain balanced against floor strongbox acquisition
Use eight strongbox tiers and a two-star ten-level augment scale as the current design direction
The strongest high-star high-augment weapons primarily come from high-tier floor strongboxes
Tier 7 and Tier 8 boxes are heavily weighted toward or nearly guarantee high-quality high-augment items
The exact weapon family and enchantments remain random even from top-tier boxes
Shops may roll exceptional two-star augment-level-seven-or-higher weapons only at screenshot-worthy rarity
Higher strongbox tiers are unavailable at low player levels and unlock progressively
Progressive box-tier availability must create continued motivation to reach and replay later levels
Exact odds guarantees level gates and availability require playtesting
Prevent both early progression-breaking jackpots and unrewarding endgame droughts
Player level and campaign progress impose a hard maximum on eligible strongbox tiers
Difficulty optional objectives bosses challenge performance and other mastery signals improve high-tier odds without bypassing progression gates
Once a strongbox tier is unlocked every level retains a non-zero chance to produce it
Earlier or easier levels are substantially less likely to produce high unlocked tiers
Earlier levels skew toward slightly weaker item-quality rolls so they do not become the optimal farming route
Later and more difficult content must provide materially better expected rewards
Exact tier weights quality penalties and performance multipliers require playtesting

recovered_state.rule: Re-verify reconstructed details before final requirements

open_questions: Mapping from eight strongbox tiers to two-star ten-level augment quality
Stack and technical architecture after product scope is mature

readiness.reason: High-weight Product Discovery decisions and recovery verification remain incomplete

next_action.instruction: Ask exactly one concise A/B/C decision card selecting whether strongbox tiers use rigid non-overlapping augment brackets, overlapping weighted quality bands with minimum guarantees, or primarily increase choice quantity rather than item quality. Preserve the accepted eight-tier and two-star ten-level direction. Put the agent recommendation after all options at the end of the card. Persist the accepted answer and update the handoff before continuing.
